content.full_text:
CITY OF ANA}IEIM, CALIFORNTA
200 South Anaheim Boulevard, Suite 356
Anaheim. California 92805
Office of
CITYATTORNEY
Telephone:
714/254-5169
FAX:
7t4/254-5123
April 6, L992
Ben Davidian, Chairman
Fair Pol-itical Practices Commission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 9581-4
Re: Disneyland Tickets
Dear Chairrnan Davidi.an:
Thank you for meeting with me and representatives of the Watt
Disney Company (rtDisney") on March 3L, L992. As you requested at
that meeting, I am following up with my questions in writing.
Disneyland is a leading California tourist attraction and is
visited by millions of guests each year. I understand that
Disneyland provides over 300,000 free admission tickets each year
to a wide variety of individuals, non-profit organizations, media
groups and charities. 
Occasionally, public officials 
(including
elected and non-elected officials of the City of Anaheim) request
complimentary tickets for their own use (for themselves and their
friends) or recommend to Disney that such tickets be provided to
charities, VfPs, community groups, constituents and other
deserving individuals. 
r am told that Disneyland receives such
requests and recommendations from public officials 
throughout the
country, including the Governorrs office, state offices, and
federal offices, among others, and not just from public officials
in the Anaheim area.
To my knowledge, the complimentary tickets provided to Anaheim
public officials for their own use (admittance for themselves and
their friends) have always been reported by such official-s as
gifts as defined by the Political Reform Act. That is, as you
recognize, not the issue. As we discussed at our meeting, fly
questions relate only to tickets which Disneyland decides to
provide to third parties who are suggested or recommended by
either members of the Anaheirn City Council or the Anaheim City
Manager or his office staff (co11ective1y the rrAnaheim public
officials" ) .


Ben Davj-dian, Chairman
Fair Po]itical Practices Cornmission
Apri] 6, 1992
Page 2
I learned at our meetinq that one or tnore public officials 
who
represent the area in which Disneyland j-s located seem to have
believed that Disneyland, in essence, has set aside a quota of
tickets for their discretionary distribution. 
As Disney
articulated at the neeting, those notions are inaccurate. Disney
explaj-ned that recomrnendations come from thousands of persons,
including public officials at aIl levels of government, media
executives and personalities, entertaj-nment executives and
personalities, charitable and community organizations, sports
teams, school leaders, etc. 
These recommendations are either
honored, rnodif ied or rejected at the discretion of Disneyland.
If Disneyland elects to provide free admission tickets to the
recoTnmended group or indi-viduaI, Disneyland leaves the tickets at
its rrWif I CaIIt' !'/indow in the name of that group or indi,vidual.
The recommending party does not make the decision as to rrhether
the tickets will be issued, does not handle the tickets, and
indeed, could not pick up the tickets if he or she wanted to do
so because identification is required by Dlsneyland to ensure
that the tickets are distributed to the correct recipient.
In addition to recommendations to provide adnission tickets to
third parties made to Disneyland by rnernbers of the Anahein City
Council, the Anaheim city Manager (or his off j,ce staff) also
makes recomrnendations to Disneyland to provide such tickets to a
variety of sources. Such reconnended recipients include
residents and businesses wj-thin Anaheim as well as residents,
businesses and public officials in other communities.
Additionally, as a convenience to Disneyland, ticket requests and
recommendations by all non-elected public official"s and employees
of the City of Anaheim are transmitted to Disneyland through the
clerical staff in the Cj-ty Managerrs office. 
The City Manager
does not personally review or approve such requests except in
unusual circurnstances, Many of the City officials 
and employees
requesting such tickets through the City Managerrs office are
also subject to the disclosure and disqual ification requirernents
of the PoIiticaI Reform Act. Disneyland currentl-y memorializes
such requests as coming from the city }lanager I s office rather
than from the recipient Anaheim officials or employees.
After the end of each calendar year, Disneyland provides to
Anaheim City Council members, and the Anaheim City Manager, a
statement which indicates the value of each Dj-sneyland special
event attended by such officials 
and the value of all regular
adrnittance tickets given to such officials for their personal- or
discretionary use. Disneyland sends this information to assist
Anahein public officials 
in preparing I'Schedule F" of their
respective annual Statements of Economic Interest. 
Recently
Disneyland has also included on this statement the number of


Ben Davidian, Chairman
Fair Pol-itical Practices Commission
Apri] 6, l-992
Page 3
tickets that were left for third partles at Disneylandts 'rwil-1
Cal1r' window in response to recommendations received from the
public officialrs 
office.
fn order to better understand how these recommendations are
treated under the Political Reform Act, I have reviewed many of
your advice letters, regulations and opinions, including a recent
letter to Assenbl]rmember Tom Umbergrs office. 
That letter, which
rr/e l-earned at our neeting was based on certain erroneous facts
provided to the Commission, seems to suggest that recornmendations
of Disneyland tickets are considered to be tickets to the
reconrnending officials 
and not the official 
who actually receives
the tickets at Disneyfandrs 'rWj-11- Calltt window and utilizes 
them
for his personal enjoyment. Since this conclusion would mean
that Disneyl-and rr/ould not be consj-dered the host of the official
who actuafly utilizes the tickets, it has caused rne some
confusion.
In reviewing other recent fetters in connection with gifts from
private corporations to horneless farnilies that were made at the
recommendation of, and/or the reconmendation of and delivery by,
public officials, 
I learned that the goods were not cons j.dered
gifts to the recornmending or delivering public officials.
Rather, they were either considered not reportabl,e at aII (under
certain circurnstances where the gifts were delivered by the
private corporatj-on rather than the official) 
or were considered
in-kind campaign contributions to the public officials 
(where the
public official 
actually delivered the qifts to the home1ess in a
manner that identified the gift as apparently corning from the
public official).
I further found some advice letters which determined that a
public official 
e/ho recommended that a gift be made to a third
party was deemed the recipient of such gift under some
circumstances whj.le other advice letters found that a public
official- naking such recommendations r^ras not the recipient of a
gift or campaign contribution under certain other circumstances.
with this array of situations j-n mind, I ask that you clarify 
how
cornplimentary tickets from Disneyland and other such event
centers should be treated under the Poli-tical Reform Act r,/hen
public officials 
reconnend that complimentary tickets be provided
to others.
Based upon the facts discussed at our rneeting on March 3:-, L992,
and articulated herein, I request your advice on the following
natters:


Ben Davj-dian, Chairman
Fair Politica1 Practices comnission
April- 6, 1992
Page 4
L. 
Are complimentary admission tickets provided directly
by Disneyland to third parties at the suggestion or
recommendation of a menber of the Anaheim City Council deemed
gifts by Di-sneyland to such City Council mernbers for purposes of
the discl-osure and disqualif ication requirenents of the PoIitical
Reform Act?
2. 
Are such complimentary tickets in-kind campaign
contributj.ons by Disney to the City Council mernbers who recommend
that such tickets be provided to a third party?
3. 
Are complimentary tickets provided directly by
Disneyland to other non-elected officiaLs or empl-oyees of Anaheim
gifts by Disney to the city l'lanager of Anaheim for purposes of
the discl-osure and disqua I i f ication requirements of the Political
Reform Act where such requests are recommended to Disneyl-and by
the city Manager or his clerical staff? Is the ans!/er different
depending upon whether the City Manager has personal, knowl-edge
of, or screens, such recommendatj-ons? Is the answer different
where the city officiaf or empl-oyee who is the recipient of such
tickets is also subject to the disclosure and disqualification
requirements of the Political Reform Act?
Because there could be lssues of possible disqual if ication if
some or afl of these Disneyland tickets that $/ere recomrnended by
Anaheim public officials in 1991 constituted gifts to such pubtic
officials, 
and because that was not and could not have been
understood when these officiaLs or their staffs made these
recommendations last year, I have recommended, as you suggested,
that each Anaheim public official 
identify the value of such
recomrnended tickets on his or her 1991 Statenents of Econonic
rnterest with an explanation that once the matter is finally
resofved, whatever anendments might be necessary or permissible
at that time will also be executed.
Finally, if the Comnission were to consj-der these reconmended
tickets to be gifts to the public officials 
who made the
recornrnendat ions, I request that these public officials 
be
permitted to reimburse Disneyland, if that is their desire, and
have such repayments deemed to be made within the thirty 
day
reirnbursement period provided by Regulation 18726.L. As r{e
discussed, the conmission has previously aIlo$/ed a tolling of
such thirty day period when the public officials 
understandably
did not realize at the time that an action constitute the receipt
of a gift for purposes of the Political Reform Act.
Again, l-et me thank you for your fast response to our request for
a meeting and for your commitment to clarify for our own public


Ben Davidian, Chairman
Fair Politi.cal Practices Conmission
April 6, 19 92
Page 5
officials 
and other public offi,cials hov/ these activities are to
be treated under the Political Reform Act.
JLW : Im

California 92805 Office of CITYATTORNEY Telephone: 714/254-5169 FAX: 7t4/254-5123 April 6, L992 Ben Davidian, Chairman Fair Pol-itical Practices Commission 428 J. Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 9581-4 Re: Disneyland Tickets Dear Chairrnan Davidi.an: Thank you for meeting with me and representatives of the Watt Disney Company (rtDisney") on March 3L, L992. As you requested at that meeting, I am following up with my questions in writing. Disneyland is a leading California tourist attraction and is visited by millions of guests each year. I understand that Disneyland provides over 300,000 free admission tickets each year to a wide variety of individuals, non-profit organizations, media groups and charities. Occasionally, public officials (including elected and non-elected officials of the City of Anaheim) request complimentary tickets for their own use (for themselves and their friends) or recommend to Disney that such tickets be provided to charities, VfPs, community groups, constituents and other deserving individuals. r am told that Disneyland receives such requests and recommendations from public officials throughout the country, including the Governorrs office, state offices, and federal offices, among others, and not just from public officials in the Anaheim area. To my knowledge, the complimentary tickets provided to Anaheim public officials for their own use (admittance for themselves and their friends) have always been reported by such official-s as gifts as defined by the Political Reform Act. That is, as you recognize, not the issue. As we discussed at our meeting, fly questions relate only to tickets which Disneyland decides to provide to third parties who are suggested or recommended by either members of the Anaheirn City Council or the Anaheim City Manager or his office staff (co11ective1y the rrAnaheim public officials" ) . Ben Davj-dian, Chairman Fair Po]itical Practices Cornmission Apri] 6, 1992 Page 2 I learned at our meetinq that one or tnore public officials who represent the area in which Disneyland j-s located seem to have believed that Disneyland, in essence, has set aside a quota of tickets for their discretionary distribution. As Disney articulated at the neeting, those notions are inaccurate. Disney explaj-ned that recomrnendations come from thousands of persons, including public officials at aIl levels of government, media executives and personalities, entertaj-nment executives and personalities, charitable and community organizations, sports teams, school leaders, etc. These recommendations are either honored, rnodif ied or rejected at the discretion of Disneyland. If Disneyland elects to provide free admission tickets to the recoTnmended group or indi-viduaI, Disneyland leaves the tickets at its rrWif I CaIIt' !'/indow in the name of that group or indi,vidual. The recommending party does not make the decision as to rrhether the tickets will be issued, does not handle the tickets, and indeed, could not pick up the tickets if he or she wanted to do so because identification is required by Dlsneyland to ensure that the tickets are distributed to the correct recipient. In addition to recommendations to provide adnission tickets to third parties made to
